Architectures](https://oneuptime.com/blog/post/2026-02-06-distributed-tracing-event-driven-architectures/view)\n2. 
[How to Correlate Asynchronous Event-Driven Flows Across Multiple 
Services](https://oneuptime.com/blog/post/2026-02-06-correlate-async-events-span-links/view)\n3. [How to Build Trace 
Correlation Strategies](https://oneuptime.com/blog/post/2026-01-30-trace-correlation-strategies/view)\n4. [How to 
Debug Data Inconsistencies in Event-Driven Systems Using OpenTelemetry](https://oneuptime.com/blog/post/2026-02-06-debu
g-data-inconsistencies-event-driven-trace-correlation/view)\n5. [How to Implement Event Metadata 
Design](https://oneuptime.com/blog/post/2026-01-30-event-metadata-design/view)\n\n# Event-Driven Architecture 
Observability: Distributed Tracing \u0026 Correlation Best Practices (2025)\n\n## Core Challenges\n\nEvent-driven 
architectures introduce unique observability challenges that differ from synchronous systems.[1] Key complexities 
include temporal decoupling (producer and consumer run at different times), fan-out patterns (single events triggering 
multiple consumers), fan-in scenarios (multiple producers triggering one action), and event replay from logs.[1]\n\n## 
Context Propagation Through Messages\n\n**W3C TraceContext Standard**: Use standardized &amp;#96;traceparent&amp;#96; and 
&amp;#96;tracestate&amp;#96; headers to propagate trace information through message brokers.[1][3] The &amp;#96;traceparent&amp;#96; 
format is: &amp;#96;00-{trace-id}-{parent-span-id}-{trace-flags}&amp;#96;, where the trace ID is a 32-character identifier 
shared across services.[3]\n\n**Implementation Pattern**: Producers inject trace context into message headers using 
OpenTelemetry, while consumers extract the context and create linked spans.[1] This enables traces to flow through 
asynchronous message brokers like Kafka and RabbitMQ.[1]\n\n## Correlation Strategies\n\n**Trace ID Propagation**: The 
foundation of correlation-a common identity travels across service boundaries so every operation in a request shares 
the same trace ID.[3]\n\n**Span Links for Asynchronous Flows**: Use OpenTelemetry span links instead of parent-child 
relationships when:[2]\n- Events lack a timing dependency\n- A batch consumer processes messages from different 
traces\n- Fan-in occurs (single span triggered by multiple upstream events)\n- Processing is delayed (messages 
produced minutes or hours ago)\n\n**Correlation IDs**: Include correlation IDs in event metadata to track related 
events throughout the entire chain, separate from OpenTelemetry trace IDs.[4]\n\n## Event Metadata 
Design\n\nComprehensive event metadata should include:[5]\n- **Identity**: Event ID, timestamp\n- **Tracing**: 
Correlation ID, causation ID, trace ID, span ID\n- **Source**: Service name, version, host, environment\n- **Schema**: 
Event type, schema version\n- **Actor Information**: User/service ID, tenant ID for audit trails\n\nThis enables 
debugging data inconsistencies where events arrive out of order, are processed multiple times, or are lost across 
distributed services.[4]\n\n## Key Tools\n\nOpenTelemetry is the recommended standard for implementing distributed 
tracing in event-driven systems.[1][2][4]",

  Here is the corrected assessment after grepping the codebase, checking &#96;pyproject.toml&#96;, and researching what 
FastStream/aiokafka provide natively:
&gt; **What is missing:** This is a **code-level event registry** (maps &#96;"user.created"&#96; to &#96;UserCreatedEvent&#96; class), 
not a **schema registry** in the Kafka sense. There is no Confluent Schema Registry, Avro/Protobuf serialization, 
forward/backward compatibility enforcement, or schema evolution governance. The &#96;EventRegistry&#96; is purely runtime 
Python class mapping - it does not prevent a consumer from receiving a broken payload because the producer removed a 
field. So my point stands partially: there is type-safe deserialization, but no schema registry for cross-service 
contract enforcement.
  
  ### 2. OpenTelemetry tracing - ? already available via FastStream, just not wired up
  
  **What exists:** FastStream &#96;0.6.7&#96; (your version) has built-in &#96;KafkaTelemetryMiddleware&#96; with OpenTelemetry 
support. Your &#96;poetry.lock&#96; shows &#96;otel = ["opentelemetry-sdk (&gt;=1.24.0,&lt;2.0.0)"]&#96; is available. The system already 
has dispatch hooks (&#96;DispatchHooks&#96; in &#96;dispatch_hooks.py&#96;) and event metadata with &#96;correlation_id&#96;/&#96;causation_id&#96;.
  
  **What is missing:** The FastStream broker in &#96;main.py&#96; (&#96;create_kafka_broker&#96;) does **not** have 
&#96;KafkaTelemetryMiddleware&#96; attached. Also, no &#96;TracerProvider&#96;, no OTLP exporter config, and no W3C TraceContext 
propagation into Kafka message headers. So the integration is **one config call away** from FastStream, but the 
scaffolding (provider setup, exporter, headers) is not wired.
  
  ### 3. Sentry integration - ? not in the codebase, but FastStream has native support
  
  FastStream has a dedicated [Sentry tracing 
page](https://faststream.ag2.ai/0.6/getting-started/observability/opentelemetry/sentry/). However, Sentry's OTel 
integration in Python is known to have limitations (context format incompatibility, shallow span cache). The 
recommended approach is to route OTel traces through an OpenTelemetry Collector that exports to Sentry. Your codebase 
has **no Sentry SDK or &#96;SentryTelemetryMiddleware&#96; configured at all**. So this would be new wiring.
  
  ### 4. Circuit breaker - ? fully missing, no library provides it
  
  Neither FastStream, aiokafka, nor your codebase has any circuit breaker pattern. FastStream has exception middleware 
and retry logic on the broker, but no closed/open/half-open circuit breaker. This is a genuine gap.

Apache Kafka (2026): A Battle-Tested Guide | NovaSphere AI 
Lab](https://www.novasphereailab.com/blog/rabbitmq-vs-kafka-2026)\n\n# RabbitMQ vs Kafka Comparison: 2025-2026 
Overview\n\n## Architecture Fundamentals\n\n**RabbitMQ** operates as a traditional message broker with a queue-based 
model where messages are deleted after consumption. It uses a push model where the broker sends messages to consumers, 
with exchanges handling routing based on routing rules.[3]\n\n**Kafka** functions as a distributed commit log for 
event streaming. It uses a pull model where consumers fetch messages, and messages are retained for configurable 
periods rather than deleted, enabling replay capabilities.[3]\n\n## Key Feature Differences\n\n**RabbitMQ 
Strengths:**[3]\n- Traditional message queue patterns (work queues, pub/sub, routing)\n- Complex routing rules and 
message priorities\n- Low latency for individual messages (\u003c10ms)\n- Simpler operational model with fewer moving 
parts\n- Message TTL and dead-letter queue support\n\n**Kafka Strengths:**[3]\n- Event streaming and event sourcing\n- 
High throughput (\u003e100k msg/s)\n- Message replay and time-travel capabilities\n- Long-term retention 
(days/weeks/years)\n- Event-driven microservices architectures\n\n## Recent Updates (2025-2026)\n\n**Kafka 4.0** 
(January 2026): Removed ZooKeeper entirely, making KRaft mode the sole consensus mechanism. Added production-ready 
tiered storage for object storage offloading and made exactly-once semantics the default.[1]\n\n**RabbitMQ 4.1** 
(February 2026): Reduced quorum queue memory consumption by ~35%, matured native streams with offset tracking and 
consumer groups, and added full MQTT 5.0 support for IoT workloads.[1]\n\n## Circuit Breaker \u0026 Rate 
Limiting\n\nThe search results do not provide specific information about circuit breaker or rate limiting features for 
either platform.\n\n## Reliability Considerations\n\nBoth systems require careful configuration to prevent failure 
scenarios. RabbitMQ users must implement dead-letter queues to avoid poison message loops during consumer crashes.[5] 
Kafka requires proper partition strategy planning and offset management, with production setup complexity being a 
significant operational burden.[5]",

  _**Agent (claude-4.5-sonnet-thinking)**_
  
  **Answer to your RabbitMQ question:** RabbitMQ is a different technology with a push-based queue model. It *does* 
have native credit-based flow control (automatic backpressure), dead letter exchanges, and per-queue message TTL/rate 
limiting. However, you cannot "add RabbitMQ" just for item 8 without rewriting the entire transport layer. Kafka and 
RabbitMQ answer different architectural needs (commit log vs. traditional message broker). Adding both simultaneously 
would be massive scope creep. **Recommendation for the plan: use aiolimiter for Kafka, and add documentation noting 
what RabbitMQ would provide natively if the transport were switched.**
&gt;       "chunk": "Links:\n1. [RabbitMQ vs Kafka: They're Not Competing - They're Complementary | Medium](https://medium
.com/@kishorchukka/rabbitmq-vs-kafka-theyre-not-competing-they-re-complementary-679e2237507a)\n2. [Kafka or RabbitMQ 
in 2025? How to pick the right one - Toxigon](https://toxigon.com/kafka-vs-rabbitmq-which-is-better-for-2025)\n3. 
[RabbitMQ vs Apache Kafka (2026): A Battle-Tested Guide | NovaSphere AI 
Lab](https://www.novasphereailab.com/blog/rabbitmq-vs-kafka-2026)\n4. [Stop Fighting Your Messaging Architecture - Use 
All Three](https://toolstac.com/integration/kafka-redis-rabbitmq/architecture-overview)\n5. [What Kafka RabbitMQ 
Actually Does and When to Use It](https://hoop.dev/blog/what-kafka-rabbitmq-actually-does-and-when-to-use-it/)\n\n# 
RabbitMQ and Kafka: Complementary Architecture Pattern\n\nRabbitMQ and Kafka are fundamentally designed to solve 
different problems and work best together rather than in competition.[1]\n\n## Core Differences\n\n**RabbitMQ** 
operates as a \"Smart Broker, Dumb Consumer\" with intelligent message routing through exchanges and queues. It 
ensures reliable, ordered delivery with acknowledgments-like a pizza delivery driver making specific 1-to-1 
deliveries.[3]\n\n**Kafka** functions as a \"Dumb Broker, Smart Consumer,\" broadcasting immutable event streams that 
multiple consumers can independently replay. It's built for high-throughput, 1-to-many distribution-like a live TV 
broadcast.[3]\n\n## Complementary Use Cases\n\nThe two systems address distinct problems in microservices 
architectures:[1]\n\n- **Choreography (Execution)**: RabbitMQ excels at reliable multi-step business processes-order 
processing, payment workflows, notifications-where sequence and delivery guarantees matter.\n- **Cohesion 
(Record-keeping)**: Kafka provides the complete, permanent audit trail across systems, enabling analytics, replay 
capability, and system observability.\n\n## Integration Pattern\n\nWhen combined, Kafka produces firehose-scale event 
data while RabbitMQ consumes it in manageable, guaranteed chunks.[5] This separation gives teams better control over 
data consistency and compliance.[5] Common practices include streaming Kafka topics to RabbitMQ queues via connectors, 
applying dead-letter queues for error handling, and monitoring Kafka partition lag as a signal for RabbitMQ worker 
scaling.[5]\n\n## When You Need Both\n\nUse this pattern when you require real-time user features, massive event 
streams, and reliable task processing simultaneously-capturing Kafka's immutable logs for analytics alongside 
RabbitMQ's dependable routing for critical workflows.[4]",
        "title": "Web search results for query: RabbitMQ Kafka together complement each other architecture pattern 
2025"
      }
    ],
    "rejected": false
  }
